content:
Numeric **feature scaling** grew alongside **multivariate statistics** and early **pattern recognition**. Before modern ML libraries, practitioners manually subtracted column means and divided by standard deviations so **principal component analysis** and **linear discriminant analysis** were not dominated by arbitrary units.

Classical **k-nearest neighbors** (Fix & Hodges, 1951; Cover & Hart, 1967) made the problem visible: without rescaling, Euclidean distance reflects whichever sensor or currency has the largest magnitude, not the scientist's intended weighting.

**Neural networks** and **support vector machines** in the 1990s–2000s reinforced standardization for **gradient descent** stability and **kernel** methods. **Ridge and Lasso** theory assumes penalties apply fairly across coefficients—motivating z-score preprocessing in penalized regression workflows.

**scikit-learn** (2010s) codified `StandardScaler` and `MinMaxScaler` inside **Pipeline** objects with explicit `fit`/`transform` separation—the same train-only discipline now standard for leakage-safe preprocessing. Tree ensembles later clarified that not every model family needs rescaling, narrowing *when to scale* to distance-, gradient-, and regularization-sensitive learners.

timeline.label: Multivariate statistics
timeline.description: Centering and standardizing columns becomes routine before PCA and related eigenmethods.

timeline.label: Instance-based learning
timeline.description: k-NN and clustering expose how raw units distort distance unless features are rescaled.

timeline.label: sklearn Pipelines
timeline.description: StandardScaler and MinMaxScaler ship with fit-on-train-only APIs bundled beside estimators.
